input_path: data\papers\Zech et al. - Model-driven Digital Twins for AECO.pdf

extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The Digital Twin provides (1) real‑time monitoring of sensor data (temperature, illumination, occupancy), (2) high‑fidelity simulation of building and lighting performance, (3) predictive analytics for energy consumption and lighting usage, (4) automated optimization of lighting set‑points and overall energy efficiency, (5) closed‑loop control actions to adjust lighting levels based on occupancy and daylight, and (6) immersive 3D visualization for interactive monitoring and scenario exploration. Services are targeted at facility managers, designers, and researchers throughout the building life‑cycle.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: Computational models are derived from enriched Building Information Models (BIM) where building control engineers define sensors, actuators, and controllers as semantic entities. The framework uses model‑driven engineering (MDE) to extract structured representations (geometric BIM data linked with behavioral control logic) and automatically generate deployable Digital Twin artifacts. Data sources originate from the defined sensor instances and are continuously synchronized through a real‑time data exchange layer. Model relationships are expressed through a unified semantic foundation that binds geometric BIM elements to behavioral models, enabling downstream transformation steps and custom service integration.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: Actuators are modelled as first‑class entities within the BIM‑based device model. Building Control Engineers define each actuator together with its associated controller in the BIM environment, enabling the DT to issue remote commands based on the embedded control logic. The framework’s modular architecture provides standardized extension points for integrating actuator drivers, allowing the Digital Twin to invoke actuation services over the real‑time data exchange layer. Specific actuator types, actuation ranges, safety constraints, or addressing schemes are not detailed in the document.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: Sensors are also captured as semantic objects in the enriched BIM device model. Engineers specify sensor type, measurement purpose, and location directly in BIM, which MDE then translates into a digital representation linked to the corresponding geometric element. The Digital Twin consumes sensor data through the real‑time data exchange capability, but the document does not enumerate sensor categories, sampling rates, precision, or communication protocols.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: The DT Framework mediates continuous, real‑time data exchange from the physical building to its digital twin. Sensor and device measurements are streamed into the twin to keep the BIM‑derived model synchronized with the building’s operational state. The modular architecture with standardized extension points enables custom services to ingest this data, and runtime configuration management allows adjustments without full redeployment.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: Control commands are generated in the digital twin (e.g., via Facility Managers’ interaction) and transmitted back through the DT Framework to the building control systems. This bidirectional exchange supports real‑time monitoring and actuation, letting the twin feed control signals to physical devices and receive confirmation of execution.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: The solution employs a layered, modular architecture that ensures interoperability across heterogeneous systems. Standardised extension points and runtime configuration management provide a flexible technical connection, while the non‑functional requirements (NF_X) address scalability, maintainability, and accessibility. No explicit network protocols, topology, bandwidth, latency, or security mechanisms are detailed in the document.
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages: The framework supports a full Digital Twin lifecycle that starts with device modeling inside the BIM environment—building control engineers define sensors, actuators and controllers to create a unified semantic foundation. Next, Model‑Driven Engineering (MDE) principles are applied to extract structured representations from the enriched BIM model and automatically generate deployable DT artefacts with minimal manual effort. The lifecycle then moves to the operational phase, where real‑time data exchange continuously synchronises the physical building with its digital counterpart. Throughout operation, the modular architecture with standardized extension points enables the integration of custom services and analytics, allowing the twin to evolve over time. Lifecycle governance is reflected in the role‑based workflow (Building Control Engineer, Facility Manager, DT Engineer) that orchestrates modeling, deployment, monitoring and extension activities.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The engineering process relies on Model‑Driven Engineering to translate enriched BIM models into executable DT artifacts. Device modeling in BIM defines semantics for sensors, actuators, and controllers, which are then extracted by MDE to produce runtime interfaces and deployment descriptors with minimal manual coding. Custom services and plugins are developed by DT Engineers and integrated through the framework’s standardized extension points, enabling incremental evolution of the twin. Model updates trigger regeneration of artifacts, supporting version control at the model level, while functional requirements (F_X) provide a basis for validating model completeness before artifact generation, ensuring quality assurance throughout the development and evolution phases.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: The twin offers continuous real‑time data exchange that keeps the digital model aligned with the physical building, and an interactive visualization interface that lets Facility Managers monitor system states and issue control actions. Modular extension points permit the addition of custom analytics services, providing processed insights that support operational decision making. Insights are delivered through the immersive user interface, though the document does not detail specific analytics techniques, confidence measures, or decision‑support workflows.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: The proposal relies on BIM as the canonical data model and exposes “standardized extension points” that define a common API for plug‑ins and custom services, ensuring interoperability between the twin and external analytics or control modules. While no specific industry standards (e.g., IEC, ISO) are enumerated, compliance is achieved through adherence to the BIM‑based data exchange paradigm and the use of a regulated modular architecture that supports certified extensions.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
I. I NTRODUCTION


Digital Twins (DT) are cyber-physical information constructs that through carefully designed integration of static
models with runtime data creates a virtual representation
of a (cyber-)physical asset (e.g., a building) for simulation, analysis, and optimization [1], [2]. Crucially, in the
architecture, engineering, construction and operations (AECO)
domain, DTs enable data-driven infrastructure management
and optimization by providing real-time insights, predictive
analytics, and enhanced decision-making capabilities [3]–[7].
This transformation promises tangible benefits such as reduced
operational costs, enhanced decision-making, and improved
stakeholder collaboration along the complete building life
cycle, from initial design to refurbishments and adaptations
during operation [2], [8]–[12]. However, the realization of this
potential substantially hinges on the ability to systematically
and efficiently deploy DTs in complex and heterogeneous
infrastructure environments [11]–[13].
Despite their promise, the deployment of infrastructure DTs
remains elusive in practice [2]. This is primarily due to
the fragmented nature of models and data, domain-specific


This research has received funding from the Austrian Research Promotion
Agency (FFG) under Grant Agreement No.: 898708, TwinLight, and Grant
Agreement No.: 910225, BOREALIS.

block_1_judge.reasoning: The document explicitly identifies the physical system used for demonstration as a 'Living Lab' within the context of building operations and lighting systems. The extraction claims it is 'Not in Document', which contradicts the source evidence.

The extraction accurately lists the services provided by the Digital Twin. 'Real-time monitoring', 'simulation', and 'life cycle-encompassing optimization' are explicitly mentioned in the abstract. 'Interactive visualization' is supported by the reference to 'Unreal Engine for rich, interactive visualization'. 'Predictive analytics' and 'control' are supported by the mention of 'real-time insights, predictive analytics' and 'feed control signals back to the BCS' in the introduction and methodology sections.

The source text clearly lists specific technologies used to implement the Digital Twin, including 'Eclipse Hono', 'Ditto', 'Unreal Engine', and 'Building Information Modeling (BIM)'. The extraction states 'Not in Document', which is factually incorrect based on the evidence provided.

I. I NTRODUCTION


Digital Twins (DT) are cyber-physical information constructs that through carefully designed integration of static
models with runtime data creates a virtual representation
of a (cyber-)physical asset (e.g., a building) for simulation, analysis, and optimization [1], [2]. Crucially, in the
architecture, engineering, construction and operations (AECO)
domain, DTs enable data-driven infrastructure management
and optimization by providing real-time insights, predictive
analytics, and enhanced decision-making capabilities [3]–[7].
This transformation promises tangible benefits such as reduced
operational costs, enhanced decision-making, and improved
stakeholder collaboration along the complete building life
cycle, from initial design to refurbishments and adaptations
during operation [2], [8]–[12]. However, the realization of this
potential substantially hinges on the ability to systematically
and efficiently deploy DTs in complex and heterogeneous
infrastructure environments [11]–[13].
Despite their promise, the deployment of infrastructure DTs
remains elusive in practice [2]. This is primarily due to
the fragmented nature of models and data, domain-specific

block_2_judge.reasoning: The extraction states 'Not in Document', but the source text explicitly discusses temporal aspects such as 'real-time data exchange' and 'live synchronization between physical assets and digital twins'. These details directly address the synchronization frequencies and temporal nature of the system.

The provided text does not contain information regarding multiple digital twin instances, hierarchical structures, or coordination mechanisms between different twins. It only mentions scalability in general terms.

The extraction states 'Not in Document', but the source text provides details on the system's orchestration and architecture, describing a 'model-driven deployment framework' where the 'DT Framework acts as a central hub' facilitating 'coordinated roles and system components'.

The extraction states 'Not in Document', but the source text mentions 'interoperability' across systems and explicitly states the framework 'integrates Eclipse Hono and Ditto' for data exchange, as well as compatibility with 'existing, AECO-specific systems and standards such as BIM'.

block_3_docs_retrieved:
TABLE I: Actor Roles in our Model-driven DT Deployment
Framework


**Role** **Description**


Building Control Engineer Models and configures devices, defines the control logic, and initiates
DT deployment from BIM models.
Facility Manager Monitors system states, interacts
with the twin, and operates the
physical infrastructure via the DT.
DT Engineer Develops and integrates custom services and plugins to extend DT
functionality.


Commensurate with the limitations of BIM as a seamless

enabler for DTs, we have developed a comprehensive set of
requirements for our model-driven deployment framework.
These requirements emerge from both theoretical considerations and practical insights gained through our research
projects, TwinLight and BOREALIS (cf. Section II).
To address the gap between BIM’s static representations and
the dynamic nature of DTs, our proposal must support the
complete life cycle of DT deployment and operation. Table II
outlines the use cases to be supported by our framework,
spanning from initial device modeling to the development
of custom DT services atop our proposal. These use cases
reflect the core functionality needed by different stakeholders
(cf. Table I) involved in the DT life cycle, including _Building_
_Control Engineers_ who model devices and implement control
logic, _Facility Managers_ who monitor and control building operations, and _DT Engineers_ who extend functionality through
custom services.

The functional requirements ( **F** _X_ ) detailed in Table III form
the conceptual foundation of our solution, beginning with
device modeling capabilities that address BIM’s fundamental
limitation in representing BCS components. This enables
building control engineers to define sensors, actuators, and
controllers directly within the BIM environment, creating a
unified semantic foundation for subsequent transformation
steps. The framework then leverages MDE principles to extract
structured representations from these enriched BIM models
and automatically generate deployable DT artifacts with minimal manual intervention.

Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime

block_3_judge.reasoning: The extracted description is highly specific and accurately reflects the model-driven approach detailed in the source. It correctly identifies the derivation of models from enriched BIM, the role of engineers in defining semantic entities (sensors, actuators), the use of MDE for extraction and artifact generation, and the real-time data synchronization. It is grounded in the text with high technical detail. Supporting quotes: 'enables building control engineers to define sensors, actuators, and controllers directly within the BIM environment', 'framework... leverages MDE principles to extract structured representations... and automatically generate deployable DT artifacts', 'integrates Eclipse Hono and Ditto for real-time data exchange'.

The extraction correctly captures the modeling of actuators as first-class entities in BIM, the role of the Building Control Engineer, and the modular extension points for actuation services. It accurately notes the absence of specific hardware details. Supported by quotes: 'Building Control Engineers who model devices and implement control logic', 'modular architecture with defined extension points to support scalable, service-oriented DT development', and 'real-time data integration using Eclipse Hono and Ditto'.

The description accurately reflects the semantic modeling of sensors within BIM and the subsequent translation via MDE, while correctly identifying the lack of specific technical specifications in the text. Supported by quotes: 'runtime semantics, such as sensor configurations... can be systematically embedded into BIM models', 'define sensors... directly within the BIM environment', and 'Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization'.

While the abstract mentions the 'Overall Digital Twin Entanglement (ODTE) metric' for evaluation, the provided text sections do not contain a description of model fidelity requirements, specific validation/verification methods, uncertainty quantification, or error detection processes as defined in the characteristic. The detailed evaluation section is not included in the source evidence.

block_4_docs_retrieved:
Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime



configuration management further enhances adaptability by
enabling adjustments without requiring full redeployment.
Complementing these functional requirements, our nonfunctional requirements ( **NF** _X_ ) address critical aspects of
interoperability across heterogeneous systems, scalability from
individual components to entire infrastructure systems, maintainability through low-overhead updates, and accessibility for
users with varying technical expertise.
This requirements-driven approach provides a systematic
foundation for implementing DTs that bridge the gap between
BIM’s static representations and the dynamic operational
needs of the AECO domain, addressing the challenges identified in Section II. At this, we want to emphasize that these
are not all requirements, but rather those pertinent to crafting a
technological spike solution tailored to the specific challenges
and innovations explored in this work (cf. Section V-C). The
discussion section will revisit these requirements to evaluate
their effectiveness and role in advancing the proposed solution.


_B. Solution Proposal_


Figure 1 (p. 6) presents the conceptual model of our
framework, illustrating how the requirements from Table III
are implemented through coordinated roles and system components. Figure 1 demonstrates the flow from enriched BIM
models to operational DTs, emphasizing the key roles and their
contributions to the DT life cycle.
_Building Control Engineers_, using BIM tools, perform
device modeling and initiate the deployment process. _Facility_
_Managers_ then interact with the deployed DT for real-time
monitoring and control. _DT Engineers_ extend the framework’s
capabilities through service integration. The DT Framework
acts as a central hub, facilitating bidirectional data exchange
and enabling a holistic approach to building life cycle management.


IV. M ODEL  - BASED D EPLOYMENT OF D IGITAL T WINS

block_6_docs_retrieved:
[11]. Most frameworks are not designed with AECO-specific
standards like BIM in mind, and those that can be adapted
— such as Bentley’s iTwin.js [32] — rely on proprietary
ecosystems or require substantial manual effort due to a lack
of automation.

Recently, Gerber et al. [33] presented an ad-hoc DT for
a Living Lab for a LEED Platinum academic building based
on BIM for monitoring, simulation, and performance analysis.
Their study emphasizes automated data acquisition, normalization, and 3D visualization, while highlighting challenges
in data integration and stakeholder coordination, insights that
directly inform model-driven approaches in DT engineering.
Their results reinforce earlier findings by Opoku et al. [34]
who also demonstrated the effective integration of DTs with
BIM and IoT platforms to optimize indoor environmental conditions in a university library setting. Both studies underscore
the significance of real-time data utilization and visualization
in enhancing facility management and highlight common challenges in data integration and stakeholder engagement in DT
implementations. In synopsis, AECO adoption is dominated by
commercial tools or custom-built solutions [2], [13]. Notably,
MDE is not present across the surveyed works, leaving a
critical gap in abstraction and reuse capabilities essential for
complex domains like AECO.
Model-driven engineering (MDE) offers a streamlined approach to handling the increasing complexity of systems by
elevating models to primary engineering artifacts [35], [36].
It enables abstraction, formalization, and automation across
the development life cycle, reducing cognitive overload and
improving consistency in large-scale systems [37]. MDE supports the definition of domain-specific modeling languages
and metamodels that unify engineering workflows, providing
a structured foundation for communication across disciplines
and life cycle phases [38], [39]. Importantly, MDE also
strengthens governance by encoding organizational policies
and architectural decisions into formal models, promoting
compliance, reuse, and quality assurance [40]. Recent advances continue to refine the integration of MDE into DT
systems [41]–[47]. As reported in Lehner et al.’s recent survey,
research primarily focuses on runtime adaptability, semantic
coherence, and tool interoperability [48]. Abbasi et al. [49]
examined the phenomenon of _semantic drift_, where the alignment between digital models and their physical counterparts
diverges over time. Their study stresses the necessity of
synchronization techniques to ensure the integrity of modelbased DTs. Kamburjan et al. [50] proposed a declarative life
cycle specification language, enabling dynamic evolution and
rule-based management of DT instances. Meanwhile, Oakes et
al. [51] advanced ontological frameworks to support service
